claim: The stacking fault energy (SFE) of the Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C steel was estimated as ~108 mJ/m^2.
evidence_text: The stacking fault energy (SFE) of the steel was estimated as ~108 mJ/m2 .

claim: The cast was homogenized at 1250 °C for 12 h and hot-forged into bars of 60 mm diameter with a finish forging temperature of ~900 °C.
evidence_text: the cast was soaked at 1250 ◦C for 12 h and then hot-forged into bars 60 mm in diameter with the finish forging temperature of ~900 ◦C,

claim: Sheets of various thicknesses (1.3, 1.7, 2.4, 4, 6 mm) were cold rolled to reductions from 10% to 90% (true strain ε = 0.1–2.3) after solution treatment at 1100 °C for 1 h.
evidence_text: The sheets with 40 mm in width, 70 mm in length and different thickness (1.3, 1.7, 2.4, 4 and 6 mm) were cut from the bars using wire electric discharge machine. The sheets with different thickness were respectively cold rolled to reduction of 10% to 90% (ε = 0.1 – 2.3) after solution treated at 1100 ◦C for 1 h.

claim: Uniaxial tensile tests were conducted at room temperature with an initial strain rate of 10^−3 s^−1; specimens were cut along the rolling direction with gauge length 16 mm and width 4 mm; three parallel tests were performed per condition.
evidence_text: The mechanical property is evaluated by uniaxial tensile test at room temperature using the SHIMADZUAG-X50KN universal testing machine with an initial strain rate of 10^-3 s^-1. The dog-bone-shaped tensile specimen is cut along the rolling direction with the gauge length of 16 mm and the width of 4 mm. Three parallel tensile tests were conducted for each condition.

claim: Microstructure evolution was investigated by optical microscopy, electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on the longitudinal section (RD×ND).
evidence_text: Microstructure evolution is investigated by optical microscopy (OM), electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) techniques on longitudinal section (RD×ND).

claim: Texture was characterized by XRD on the RD×TD plane using incomplete pole figures {111}, {200}, {220}, and orientation distribution functions were calculated by the Arbitrarily Defined Cells method.
evidence_text: The texture evolution was characterized by x-ray diffraction (XRD) using a Rigaku D/MAX 2500PC x-ray diffractometer. The incomplete pole figure {111}, {200} and {220} were measured on the RD×TD plane. The orientation distribution functions (ODFs) and Fmax were calculated by the Arbitrarily Defined Cells method.

claim: The as-quenched steel exhibited yield strength ≈570 MPa, ultimate tensile strength ≈830 MPa, and uniform elongation exceeding 75%, indicating excellent work hardening capability.
evidence_text: In the as-quenched state, the steel was characterized by a low yield strength (YS) and ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of ~570 MPa and ~ 830 MPa, respectively. But it exhibited a dramatic uniform elongation (UE) of exceeding 75%, which meant the excellent work hardening capability.

claim: Yield strength increased with cold rolling reduction, reaching ~2.0 GPa at 90% reduction (approximately 3.5 times the as-quenched state), while uniform elongation decreased due to loss of work hardening capability.
evidence_text: With the increase of cold rolling reduction, the YS of material gradually increased and eventually reached to ~2.0 GPa that is approximate 3.5 times of the as-quenched state. Simultaneously, the materials lost its work hardening capability, resulting to the decrease of the UE.

claim: The as-quenched microstructure consisted of austenite and nano-sized κ'-carbides uniformly distributed in the matrix; κ'-carbides formed via spinodal decomposition due to high Al and C content.
evidence_text: The phase constituent was estimated as austenite and κ’-carbides, and the nano-sized κ’-carbides were uniformly distributed in the austenitic matrix. These κ’-carbides are formed via spinodal decomposition during quenching, which is attributed to the high precipitate kinetics caused by the high Al and C content.

claim: The as-quenched microstructure contained abundant annealing twins and had an average grain size (including twins) of ~70 μm, with a nearly random texture.
evidence_text: The microstructure contained a large amount of annealing twins, and the grain size (including twins) is ~70 μm. In addition, the texture was almost random distribution.

claim: Deformation twins with an average thickness of ~5.1 nm were detected at 50% reduction, and diffraction contrast variations within a single twin indicated high dislocation density.
evidence_text: Moreover, in some grains, the intermittent linear structures in parallel arrangement were detected, as shown in Fig. 5(b). According to the corresponding selected area diffraction pattern (SADP) (Fig. 5(c)) and the dark-field TEM micrograph (Fig. 5(d)), they turned out to be deformation twins. These twins were very fine with an average thickness of ~5.1 nm. Furthermore, it should be noted that different diffraction contrast was found in the single twin, which means the high dislocation density in twin [31].

claim: The selected orientation distribution functions (ODF) sections of φ2 = 0°, 45°, 65° are shown in Fig. 8.
evidence_text: The selected orientation distribution functions (ODF) sections of $\boldsymbol { \varphi } 2 = 0 ^ { \circ } , 4 5 ^ { \circ } , 6 5 ^ { \circ }$ are shown in Fig. 8.

claim: In τ-fiber, the intensity of Cu orientation rapidly increased to maximum at 50% rolling reduction and then decreased, while the Goss orientation increased rapidly and Φ shifted to higher values.
evidence_text: The intensity of the Cu orientation rapidly increased to maximum at 50% rolling reduction and then decreased. ... the Goss orientation also increased rapidly.

claim: When true stress is lower than ~1.2 GPa, microstructure evolution is characterized by refinement of slip band spacing in Taylor lattice (Fig. 4), associated with dislocation planar glide from 'glide plane softening' effects.
evidence_text: When the true stress lower than \~1.2 GPa, the microstructure evolution is characterized by the refinement of the slip band spacing in the Taylor lattice (Fig. 4), ... The Taylor lattice structure is associated with the dislocation planar glide, which is widely considered as the results of "glide plane softening" effects [12,29].

claim: When stress reaches ~1.5 GPa, microstructure of microbands and their intersections is well established (Fig. 5(a)), and nano-sized deformation twins are detected (Fig. 5(b-d)), with microband boundaries composed of geometrically necessary dislocations.
evidence_text: When the stress reaches \~1.5 GPa, microstructure of microbands and its intersections are well established except the further refinement of the slip bands (Fig. 5(a)), ... Additionally, the novel nano-sized deformation twins are detected, as shown in Fig. 5 (b-d) ... The boundaries of the microbands are geometrically necessary dislocations (GNDs) that are formed to accommodate the misorientation between adjacent boundaries [12].

claim: The critical shear stress for deformation twinning calculated as τc = (2 γSF) / bp is ~1.48 GPa (γSF ≈108 mJ/m², bp ≈0.146 nm), much higher than the maximum resolved shear stress ~0.75 GPa, yet twinning occurs.
evidence_text: the stacking fault energy (SFE) of Fe–Mn–Al–C austenitic steel increases significantly ... τc = (2 γSF) / bp ... The SFE, bp of current steel are estimated as ~108 mJ/m2 and 0.146 nm, respectively. Therefore, the critical stress is calculated as ~1.48 GPa. It is much higher than the maximum resolved shear stress ~0.75 GPa. However, the twining indeed occurs.

claim: Twinning occurs due to (I) the actual critical shear stress being lower when dislocation–short range order (SRO) interaction is considered, and (II) inhomogeneous strain distribution leading to local stress concentration promoting twinning.
evidence_text: There are two causes to explains this phenomenon. (I) The actual critical shear stress was reported to be lower than that calculated in eq. (1) when the interaction between dislocation and short range order (SRO) is taken into account. (II) The inhomogeneous distribution of strain leads to the local stress concentration, which may also promote the twining process.

claim: At ~1.8 GPa, much microshear bands are generated due to localized strain concentration (Fig. 6(b)), and at ~2.1 GPa, dislocation cell structure is clearly observed (Fig. 6(c)), indicating cross slip operation.
evidence_text: When the stress increases to ~1.8 GPa, much microshear bands are generated due to the larger localized strain concentration (Fig. 6(b)). ... at stress of ~2.1 GPa, dislocation cell structure is clearly observed (Fig. 6(c)), indicating that dislocation cross slip is operated.

claim: Taylor lattice strengthening is attributed to long-range interaction between coplanar slip bands rather than dynamic Hall-Petch effects, with σTL = K·M·G·b/D, where D is the average slip band distance.
evidence_text: the strengthening mechanism of Taylor lattice is attributed to the long range interaction between the coplanar slip bands rather than the dynamic Hall-Petch effects [14]. The strengthening effect (σTL) is expressed by ... σTL = K · M · G · b / D

claim: The latter microbands, twins, shear bands and dislocation tangles enhance strength by Hall-Petch effects, and eventually the ultrahigh yield strength of ~2.0 GPa is realized.
evidence_text: The latter microbands, twins, shear bands and dislocation tangles enhance the strength by the Hall-Petch effects. Eventually, the ultrahigh yield strength of ~2.0 GPa is realized.

claim: Table 1 defines the Miller indices, Euler angles, and fiber affiliations for observed texture components: Brass {110}⟨112⟩, Goss {110}⟨001⟩, Cu {112}⟨111⟩, CuT {552}⟨115⟩, S {123}⟨634⟩, E {111}⟨110⟩, F {111}⟨112⟩, etc.
evidence_text: Table 1 Definition of texture component illustrated in Fig. 7. (texture components and angles listed)

claim: When deformation twinning is activated, the copper-to-brass texture transition proceeds through Cu grains twinning to CuT, followed by rotation of isolated twins to G orientation around ⟨110⟩ by coplanar slip, while in twin-matrix lamellae dislocation constraint causes abnormal rotation and eventually micro shear bands with orientations biased towards G and B.
evidence_text: Twining occurs firstly in the grains with Cu orientation, making orientation change to CuT. If the morphology of twins is isolated, the twins will rotate to G orientation around ⟨110⟩ axis by dislocation glide on the two coplanar systems... Eventually, the twining plane (also the dislocation glide plane) is nearly parallel to the rolling plane... micro shear bands are generated by local strain concentration. These shear hands are reported to consist of tiny grains, and the orientation of the grains is biased towards G and B. The G finally transformed to B through further dislocation slip.

claim: Both dislocation planar slip and deformation twinning, accompanied by microshear bands from local strain concentration, contribute to the transition from copper-type to brass-type texture in Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C steel.
evidence_text: In present work, the dislocation planar slip (as in Cu–Mn alloy) dominates the deformation. In addition, deformation twins (as in Cu–Zn alloy and TWIP steel etc.) are also detected... In conclusion, above two mechanisms may be both contributed to the transition from “Copper-type texture” to “Brass-type texture”.
